quote: When electrical current flows through a conductor placed within a magnetic field, a mechanical push is created. This interaction follows Fleming's left-hand rule. The rotational force scales directly with electrical input, meaning torque is proportional to the current.

Magnets supply the invisible field lines necessary for the electromagnetic interaction. This field intensity, measured in Tesla or Gauss, determines how much mechanical force the system can produce. The interaction is governed by F = BLI.

A reluctance motor is a type of electric motor that induces non-permanent magnetic poles on the stator and rotor. It runs synchronously at the same rate as the stator field. A switched reluctance motor can run without permanent magnets because it uses a ferromagnetic rotor that has no windings, and torque is produced by changing magnetic reluctance.
Switched reluctance motors may have high torque ripple and noise—especially at low speed.

Switched reluctance motors function via switching the current in the stator windings to attract and repel the rotor poles, which are made of ferromagnetic material. They are noted for their simple construction and ruggedness.

Switched reluctance motor (SRM) presents the absence of windings on the rotor, rugged structure, adaptability to harsh environment conditions, tolerance to faults, and simple construction, which are some of the desirable characteristics required for electric vehicles (EVs) and robotic arm applications. However, it exhibits low torque density, high torque pulsations, and low average torque.

The VMSM eliminates traditional rotor magnets by relying on software and power electronics to generate its magnetic field instead. It generates and controls the rotor's magnetic field in real time using power electronics and proprietary control algorithms while maintaining a brushless, slip-ring-free design.

Vimag asserts the platform matches or exceeds permanent-magnet performance without the magnets, though independent production-scale validation is absent. Industry context notes that magnet-free systems typically sacrifice efficiency for peak power, directly affecting EV range.

A recent German engineering approach replaces slip rings with a shaft-integrated inductive coupling for separately excited synchronous motors. This reduces losses by about 15% over conventional techniques while shrinking the motor's axial length by roughly 90 millimeters. Eliminating slip rings removes the need for sealed dry compartments and enables direct oil cooling for the rotor.

Synchronous reluctance variants generate torque through magnetic alignment rather than permanent fields, always requiring variable frequency drives. Squirrel-cage induction machines have dominated industrial applications for over a century by inducing rotor currents via alternating stator fields instead of direct excitation.

The rotors contain no conductors in the rotor and operate with no permanent magnets. High efficiency: ability to meet IE4 and future standards. Robustness and high power density. No brushes, no conductors in the rotor, no rare earths. No overheating in the rotor. Low rotor inertia and high dynamic response.

In electric vehicle (EV) applications, hybrid excitation of switched reluctance motors (HESRMs) are gaining popularity due to their advantages over other EV motors. The benefits include control flexibility, simple construction, high torque/power density, and the ability to operate over a broad speed range.

~95% of all EVs use Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors (PMSM) — all require NdFeB. A typical single-motor BEV contains ~550g REO. A dual-motor performance BEV: up to 1,650g+. China controls >90% of Nd, Pr, Dy, Tb refined supply. NdPr surged +105% to ~$100/kg in 2026.

Every 100 kW of PMSM power requires 1.2 kg NdFeB magnets in the motor. A 150 kW single-motor BEV = ~1.05 kg REO just for the traction motor. A conventional ICE car contains ~140g of rare earth oxides. A typical single-motor BEV contains ~550g of rare earth oxides.
Electret and electrostatic motors do not require magnets and represent some of the earliest electrical motion devices. Piezoelectric actuators also generate movement without magnetic fields.

Vimag Labs secured its fifth Indian patent for an electric motor that runs without any rare-earth magnets, using software and power electronics to generate its magnetic field. The patent is covered by A Robust Rotating Transformer Excited Synchronous Motor and Its Control. Developed over over 87,600 engineering hours. Ten additional patents and fifteen trademarks are currently pending.

Pilot programs are underway with two-wheeler and passenger vehicle manufacturers. The company targets industrial systems from 200 kW to 600 kW, plus robotics, defense, and cooling applications. Vimag recently closed a $5 million Series A round and established a manufacturing partnership with Jendamark.
